div.head: The Chargé in the United Kingdom (Gallman) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 246. 1. Both LaithwaiteSir Gilbert Laithwaite, British Deputy Under Secretary of State for Burma. and SmithF. W. H. Smith, British Acting Assistant Under Secretary of State for Burma. expressed fervent hopes last night at impressive British Govt reception for Burmese leaders that talks with Burmese leaders will be successful. Smith said that at second meeting yesterday British suggested division of work among committees. (Emb’s 228, January 13.Not printed; it reported that the first meeting was “purely formal.” (845C.00/1–1347)) Burmese replied that they preferred to operate as single group, implication being, according to Smith, that members Burmese delegation desire to watch each other.

2. Aung SanHead of the Burma Mission to London and Deputy Chairman of the Burmese Executive Council; also Counselor for Defense and External Affairs and President of the Anti-Fascist People’s Freedom League (AFPFL) of Burma. and Tin TutCounselor for Finance of the Burmese Executive Council and member of the Burma Mission to London. spoke cordially of PackerEarl L. Packer, Consul General at Rangoon. and of interest in Burmese problems. They promised to get in touch with Embassy when talks are further advanced.
3. Dalton, Chancellor of Exchequer, was overheard to say to Aung San that he should come along to British Treasury for talks “after everything else has been decided”.
4. Temperate character Aung San’s response to Attlee’sClement R. Attlee, British Prime Minister. speech of welcome pleased both Laithwaite and Smith.
